title: Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST CSF RS.AN-01 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF RS.AN-01 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.41: Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.41' running on snow:incident, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `snow:incident` — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-90d state=7
| where isnull(u_root_cause_code) OR u_root_cause_code=""
| stats count by assignment_group
description: Ensures analysts filled root-cause codes when closing incidents for after-action analysis quality.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:incident. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Make field mandatory in workflow; (2) train SOC; (3) export for RC.IM. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:incident`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned security control testing** — Scheduled penetration tests, red team exercises, and vulnerability assessments conducted by authorised security teams generate control deviations that are expected as part of the continuous improvement programme.

2. **Risk acceptance decisions** — The CISO or risk committee may formally accept certain residual risks with documented compensating controls, resulting in control gaps that are acknowledged and tracked through the risk register rather than requiring remediation.

3. **Technology migration periods** — During approved system migrations, decommissioning activities, or technology refresh cycles, temporary control coverage gaps occur that are documented in the project risk assessment and approved by the steering committee.

4. **Capability maturity baseline adjustments** — When the organisation revises its target CSF profile or adjusts maturity targets based on business strategy changes, historical measurements against the prior baseline produce expected variances.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_csf_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved testing windows, accepted risk IDs from the risk register, and migration project identifiers. The correlation search filters results against this lookup and suppresses events where `approved_exception=true` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `RS.AN-01`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST CSF RS.AN-01 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.41: Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Make field mandatory in workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 2:** train SOC. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** export for RC.IM. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 90 days, grouped by `assignment_group`. For "Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `assignment_group` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(assignment_group) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.7.41: Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.7.41,reg=NIST_CSF
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assignment_group`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assignment_group
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.7.41: NIST CSF evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Distribution (the key fields):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST CSF-specific failure modes:**

• Tier calibration: NIST CSF defines four Implementation Tiers (Partial, Risk Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive). Detections tuned for Tier 4 (Adaptive) organisations over-report on Tier 1-2 organisations still establishing risk processes. Tag the UC's intended Tier in metadata; adjust thresholds per organisational maturity.

• CSF 2.0 'Govern' function: added in version 2.0 (Feb 2024), covering governance and supply-chain risk. UCs authored against CSF 1.1 do not explicitly touch Govern — reconcile the UC set annually against the current subcategory list.

• Profile alignment: CSF Profiles (current and target) describe an organisation's prioritised outcomes. A detection that doesn't map to the current Profile produces uncontextualised noise. Reference the Profile ID in the UC; revisit when the profile is updated.

• Informative References: NIST CSF maps subcategories to NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, COBIT etc. Detections should not claim framework coverage solely on the strongest mapping — if the CSF subcategory lists multiple frameworks, the UC's evidence should be usable for all of them.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework tells us what good practice looks like, and we keep evidence we match it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Root Cause Field Completeness on Closed Incidents' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NIST CSF control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NIST CSF control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NIST CSF control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NIST CSF programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
